terms.definition: An algorithm design paradigm that breaks a problem into smaller subproblems, solves each independently, and combines the results.
terms.example: Merge Sort splits an array in half (divide), recursively sorts each half (conquer), then merges them (combine).

terms.definition: The operation that combines two sorted subarrays into a single sorted array in O(n) time using two pointers.
terms.example: Merging [3, 27, 38] and [9, 43] produces [3, 9, 27, 38, 43] by always picking the smaller of the two current elements.

terms.definition: A sorting algorithm that preserves the original relative order of elements that compare as equal.
terms.example: If two records share the same salary, a stable sort keeps them in the same order they appeared before sorting.

terms.definition: A temporary array used during the merge step to hold a copy of the elements being merged.
terms.example: Before merging indices [low..high], values are copied to aux[low..high]; the merge writes sorted results back to arr.

terms.definition: A tree diagram showing all recursive calls, with each node representing one subproblem.
terms.example: For Merge Sort on n=8, the recursion tree has 4 levels (log₂ 8), with 8 leaf calls each handling a single element.

terms.definition: The condition at which recursion stops — an array of length 0 or 1 is already sorted and requires no further splitting.
terms.example: mergeSort([5]) returns immediately because a single-element array is trivially sorted.

terms.definition: A sort that uses O(1) extra memory beyond the input array, rearranging elements without a separate auxiliary buffer.
terms.example: Quick Sort is in-place; Merge Sort is not because it requires an O(n) auxiliary array for the merge step.

terms.definition: A sorting technique designed for datasets too large to fit in RAM, using disk storage for intermediate runs.
terms.example: Merge Sort is the basis for external sorting: sort chunks that fit in memory, write them to disk, then K-way merge.

terms.definition: Merging K sorted sequences simultaneously using a min-heap that always yields the globally smallest remaining element.
terms.example: Merging 4 sorted disk files uses a 4-element heap; each extraction takes O(log 4) = O(1) time, total O(n log K).

terms.definition: A pair of indices (i, j) where i < j but arr[i] > arr[j] — a measure of how unsorted an array is.
terms.example: In [3, 1, 2], the inversions are (3,1) and (3,2) — two inversions total. A sorted array has zero inversions.

terms.definition: An iterative variant that starts by merging pairs of single elements, then pairs of two-element runs, doubling run size each pass.
A sorting algorithm whose decisions depend entirely on pairwise comparisons of elements, subject to the Ω(n log n) lower bound.
terms.example: Merge Sort, Quick Sort, and Heap Sort are all comparison-based; Counting Sort and Radix Sort are not.

terms.definition: The theoretical minimum number of comparisons any comparison-based sort must make in the worst case to sort n elements.
terms.example: A decision tree for n elements has n! leaves; a binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves, so h ≥ log₂(n!) ≈ n log n.

terms.definition: A hybrid sort used in most standard library implementations that starts with Quick Sort and switches to Heap Sort when recursion depth exceeds a threshold.
terms.example: Python's Timsort (a Merge Sort variant) and C++'s std::sort (Introsort) both avoid O(n²) worst cases.

terms.definition: A hybrid sorting algorithm derived from Merge Sort and Insertion Sort, used in Python and Java, that detects natural runs in the input for efficiency.
terms.example: Python's list.sort() uses Timsort; on nearly sorted data it approaches O(n) because it merges fewer, longer natural runs.

terms.definition: A sorted segment of the input written to disk during the first phase of external merge sort.
terms.example: To sort a 10 GB file with 1 GB RAM, create 10 sorted runs of 1 GB each, then K-way merge them.

terms.definition: A linked list technique that uses two pointers advancing at different speeds to find the midpoint in a single pass.
terms.example: In merge sort on a linked list, the slow pointer advances one step and the fast pointer two; when fast reaches the end, slow is at the midpoint.
